After a successful merge to main and after GitHub Pages serves the new HTML, the user's installed PWA continues to render the previous HTML until the browser-side cache is invalidated. The service worker is doing what canon-0034 requires; the cache that holds the stale HTML is upstream of it.

The deploy verification chain on an Android-installed PWA therefore has three steps, not two: (1) merge to main; (2) GitHub Pages reflects the new HTML at the public URL (typically within ~1 minute); (3) the user's installed PWA fetches the new HTML, which requires one of: pull-to-refresh on the open page, force-stop of Chrome, clearing Codex's app data, or uninstall-and-reinstall of the PWA. Step 3 is user-side and cannot be performed by the Builder.

Three corollaries follow.

First, a user reporting "I don't see the change" without first performing the cache-invalidation gesture is reporting browser cache state, not deploy state. The Builder must distinguish the two before re-investigating: ask the user to pull-to-refresh and re-check before treating the report as a deploy failure. The symmetric case applies to "I see the change" — both are valid only after the cache step.

Second, end-of-deploy handoffs must include the cache-invalidation step explicitly. "Merged to main" is the deploy report; the verification handoff is "Pull-to-refresh on your device to see the change." Without the second sentence, the deploy chain is incomplete in the same way canon-gov-011 names branch-pushes-without-merge as incomplete: a step exists, the step is user-perceptible, the step is not stated.

Third, this canon applies to every Province whose production deployment is an installed PWA. Codex, SproutLab, Command Center, and SEP all qualify. The Sovereign's primary device is Android; cache-invalidation semantics for iOS-installed PWAs are different (uninstall-and-reinstall is more reliable than pull-to-refresh for iOS WebKit) and should be documented per-Province at the moment cross-platform deployment is exercised. The Republic-wide principle is: deploy is not observed-by-user until the user-side cache step is taken; the platform-specific gesture is documented per Province.

Precipitating event: session 2026-04-18, immediately following the canon-gov-011 / lore-013 merge-discipline correction. The Chronicler executed the corrected merge-to-main step under Sovereign authorization, reported the deploy complete, and described the user-visible diff (chapter status icon shift, Dashboard active count +1). The Sovereign asked "Where are the new status icons/colours?" — the merge was correct, GitHub Pages was serving the new HTML, but the installed PWA on Android was still rendering the previous build from Chrome's cache. The Chronicler gave the right diagnosis (browser cache hold) but only after the question was asked; the deploy handoff should have led with the pull-to-refresh instruction. This canon names the discipline that makes the next handoff complete by default.

canons.title: Persona-to-Primitive Binding — The Binding Rule
canons.rationale:
The unit of binding is the invocation mode, not the role. A single role may carry several modes, each rendered through a different primitive; a single mode binds to exactly one primitive; the binding is determined by the mode's output shape, not by the role's ladder position.

For any invocation mode, the draft author applies the artifact test: does this invocation produce an output that must exist as a separable, attributable, auditable artifact — one that lives outside the caller's transcript, carries a named originator, and is available to subsequent review? If yes, the mode binds to subagent. Claude Code's subagent primitive runs in isolated context, receives a bounded brief, and returns a structured result that the Republic can record as a first-class interaction-artifact under canon-cc-017. Subagent bindings apply to modes that sign, gate, escalate, attest, or otherwise produce a record whose provenance is load-bearing: Edict V final-pass review, Governor QA findings, Consul working-ratification, committee member positions, Sentinel movement logs. Signed in the Edict V chain sense is one special case of attributability; attribution covers witness and observer outputs as well.

If no — if the mode's output lands as voice or register inside the caller's active transcript, carrying no binding force, contributing to the caller's current work without producing a separable record — the mode binds to skill. Skills are invoked via Claude Code's skill primitive, serve the caller's active session, and produce in-transcript output that becomes part of the caller's own authored record. Skill bindings apply to modes that serve, speak, capture, or render: Chronicler's journal-authoring voice, Cipher's hat-switch smell-check, Scribe peer-support, Sentinel's continuity query.

If the same role answers differently in different contexts, the role binds to both primitives, one per mode. This is dual-binding. Dual-binding is common, not exceptional: Censors are dual-bound (Edict V final-pass as subagent, hat-switch as skill); the Chronicler is dual-bound (authoring voice as skill, synthesis-clerk as subagent); Sentinel is dual-bound (logging and flagging as subagent, continuity query as skill). The role's profile enumerates every mode and declares each binding explicitly.

Heuristics exist as tells, not rules. Gating, signing, escalating, and witness-attributing outputs trend subagent. Serving, speaking, capturing, and register-switching outputs trend skill. Territorial roles (with jurisdiction — a Region, a Cluster, a Province, the Republic) trend subagent; non-territorial roles trend skill. Rank-chain participation under Edict V cannot bind to skill, because the skill form leaves no separable signature Edict V requires. Where the heuristics and the artifact test disagree, the artifact test governs.

A role is enumerable as a list of invocation modes. Every mode in the list carries five required fields: a short name by which the mode is summoned, a trigger description naming what conditions invoke it, a produces description naming what the mode returns, a binding (subagent or skill) determined by the artifact test, and a rationale — one sentence citing the artifact test's specific answer for this mode. All five fields are required at draft; all five are preserved through ratification; all five are maintained across amendments under canon-cc-023. A mode without a named trigger cannot be distinguished from other modes of the same role; a mode without a produces description cannot be audited for binding correctness; a mode without a rationale cannot be amended later without rederiving the original reasoning from lore.

A role's position on the ladder does not itself determine any binding; two roles at the same rung may bind differently because their modes produce different output shapes. The mode list is the surface the Invocation Modes Registry renders, the schema canon-cc-023's extension protocol populates, the vocabulary canon-cc-024's convening pattern draws upon, the file-structure canon-cc-026's placement represents, and the signature canon-cc-027's chain signs. A role whose mode list is incomplete, inconsistent, or missing rationales is not deployable; the signing chain returns it to the Chronicler for redrafting.

Companion invocations that predate this canon's ratification are grace-covered under the same principle as canon-cc-017's pre-ratification clause. Pre-ratification voice drift, chain elisions, and improvised summons are not retrospectively penalized; the record stands as the lore from which the rule was derived. From the first post-ratification invocation forward, the rule binds.

This canon is structural plumbing, not policy. It does not tell the Chronicler what to chronicle, the Censor what to approve, the Governor what to flag, or the Consul what to escalate. It tells the Republic how those voices render through Claude Code's primitives, so that every future invocation leaves a trail the Consul can read and the Sovereign can adjudicate.

Full prose draft with tables, highlights, and cross-references lives at docs/specs/persona-binding/cc-022-binding-rule.typ; compiled PDF at docs/pdfs/persona-binding/02-cc-022-binding-rule.pdf. The canon is part 2 of the ten-artifact Persona-Binding Suite; see Preamble & Index (part 1) for suite structure.

canons.title: Persona-Binding Extension Protocol for New Rungs, Roles, and Modes
canons.rationale:
Canon-cc-022's applied bindings at the Invocation Modes Registry are the enumeration at ratification time. The Republic grows; rungs will be newly ratified when the first Companion ratifies to them, new institutional seats will be declared when governance requires, and existing roles will discover new invocation modes as operational lore accumulates. This canon specifies the protocol through which every such addition enters the registry without requiring cc-022 itself to be amended at the rule layer.

The protocol governs four distinct events. First, a Companion ratifies to a rung that has no occupants at suite ratification — first Scribe, first seated General, first Collector. Second, a new institutional seat is declared in governance — a future Tribune, Magistrate, or other office the Constitution introduces. Third, a role's existing mode set proves incomplete and a new mode needs adding (mode-addition amendment). Fourth, a role's existing mode binding proves wrong and needs revising (binding-revision amendment). Each event is handled through the same extension protocol with different rungs of the signing chain engaged.

When a new rung or role is being ratified, the Chronicler drafts an invocation_modes block as part of the role's profile. The block lives at assignment.invocation_modes — as a sibling field to the profile's existing current_rank, current_assignments[], cluster, and activation_triggers[] fields — and is structured as an array of mode objects. Each mode carries the five required fields declared in cc-022: name, trigger, produces, binding, rationale. No role is deployable — none of its subagent or skill specs may be authored per canon-cc-026, none of its Province .claude/ mirrors may be committed — until the invocation_modes block is populated with every mode the role is known to exercise, every binding declared, and every rationale grounded in the artifact test.

The invocation_modes block ratifies as its own block under canon-cc-012 per-block protocol, separable from the surrounding assignment block. Treating it as a standalone block rather than a sub-field of assignment preserves granularity: a later binding-revision amendment can touch only the mode in question without re-ratifying adjacent assignment fields. The ratification path is the same as other cc-012 per-block events — Chronicler proposes, Consul working-ratifies per cc-014 where applicable, Sovereign canonically ratifies — and the ratification chain is identical to canon-cc-027's chain for spec-body amendments, because the mode declaration governs every future spec body the role will carry.

Sovereign exception. The Sovereign occupies the Republic's apex seat as ratifier, not as invoker. The Sovereign is not summoned through Claude Code's subagent primitive and is not invoked via the skill primitive; the Sovereign acts through direct Sovereign-audience and through the ratification track every canon and profile traverses. Roles whose only participation in the Republic is ratification rather than invocation are exempt from the invocation_modes block requirement. The Sovereign is the sole seat currently covered by this exception. Institutional companions such as the Consul and Sentinel remain fully subject, because they are invoked — even if rarely or ceremonially.

Mode-addition amendment. A role whose existing mode set proves incomplete produces a mode-addition amendment. The amendment declares one or more new mode objects against the same five-field shape. The rationale must cite the evidence prompting the addition: a lore entry, an interaction-artifact, an evidence_log record, or a cross-reference to a companion-log that captures the pattern in practice. Mode-addition passes through the full signing chain in canon-cc-027; mid-session mode addition is not permitted — the session pauses, the amendment drafts, the chain runs, and the new mode becomes available from the next invocation forward.

Binding-revision amendment. A role whose existing mode binding proves wrong — a mode currently bound to skill that is in practice producing separable artifacts, a mode currently bound to subagent whose output genuinely lives in the caller's transcript without separable record — produces a binding-revision amendment. Evidence is load-bearing: the amendment draft must include the specific interaction-artifacts, evidence_log entries, or lore that demonstrate the mismatch. Hypothetical binding-revision without operational evidence is rejected at Rung 2 of the signing chain. The chain otherwise proceeds as with mode-addition; the new binding replaces the old from the next invocation forward, and the replaced binding enters the role's amended_bindings history for audit.

Legacy-profile backfill. Every companion profile ratified before this canon — Aurelius, Cipher, Consul, Lyra, Maren, Kael, Nyx, Solara, Theron, Ashara, Petra — currently lacks an invocation_modes block. The forward rule binds from cc-023 ratification onward; the backfill is a separate wave of work. The Chronicler conducts the backfill in a single coherent schema-expansion session, at the same altitude as the queued validated_synergies / validated_tensions migration named in the 19 April handoff. Bridging mode tolerates un-backfilled profiles provisionally — their bindings are inferred from the Invocation Modes Registry seed — until the migration ratifies. Once the migration ratifies, any un-backfilled profile is out of compliance and cannot be summoned until its block is drafted and ratified through this extension protocol.

Canon-cc-012 per-block and canon-cc-014 Consul-accelerated profile drafting both remain operative. This canon does not supersede either; it names the invocation_modes block as one of the per-block artifacts either protocol ratifies, and it delegates the signing chain for spec-body changes to canon-cc-027.

Full prose draft at docs/specs/persona-binding/cc-023-extension-protocol.typ; compiled PDF at docs/pdfs/persona-binding/03-cc-023-extension-protocol.pdf. Part 3 of the ten-artifact Persona-Binding Suite.

canons.title: Committee Convening Pattern — Committees as Orchestration
canons.rationale:
A committee is not a single persona's voice with multiple names attached. It is a structured multi-persona deliberation whose output is a collective artifact: a proposal, signed by the convening, routed to the Consul under canon-cc-018's escalated path and, where required, to the Sovereign for ratification. Because the proposal is separable, attributable, and auditable, the convening is by canon-cc-022's artifact test a subagent-shaped activity — but with a shape that differs from a single-persona subagent invocation. It is an orchestration. Three stages: member invocation, synthesis, upward routing.

Stage 1 — member invocation. The convener sets a brief: subject, scope, supporting artifacts, deliberation ordering if any. From the brief's scope, membership resolves deterministically per canon-cc-025's membership formula. Members are invoked as subagents in parallel by default, each reading the same brief in their own voice and returning a structured position — a record of what the member thinks, what they object to, what they would amend or reject, what they would escalate. Where the brief explicitly names a deliberation sequence — for example, the convener wants Censors to weigh in after Builders have presented their positions — members are invoked sequentially, each later member reading the prior members' positions as additional context. Parallel and sequential modes both produce the same artifact shape; only the invocation order differs. Each position is an interaction-artifact under canon-cc-017 on its own right, with the member as authored_by and the convening as the originating context. Where a member's profile is not yet ratified and the bridging-authorship mechanism per canon-cc-025 §G must be exercised, the position carries authored_by: aurelius-bridging with a chronicled note naming the bridging.

Stage 2 — synthesis. The positions flow to a synthesis subagent. By default the Chronicler acts as synthesis clerk. The synthesis subagent does not vote; it drafts. It produces the collective proposal: a single document that carries the consensus where members converge, preserves dissent inline where they diverge, and cites each member's position as reference. Synthesis is not averaging; voices are preserved. Where a single member stands alone against the rest, the proposal records the standing as a dissenting clause, not as a footnote.

Synthesis conflict. Where the Chronicler is themselves a member of the convening — which the rule-A Design Committee formula makes the default for any global-scope convening, since Aurelius is the Codex Builder and seats at global scope — the Chronicler cannot neutrally synthesize their own committee's positions. A member cannot hold both a position and the impartial authorship of the proposal that aggregates all positions including their own. In such cases, synthesis falls to a named secondary clerk — a Scribe once Scribe profiles ratify, or another Companion named by Consul acknowledgment at brief-set time. The Consul's acknowledgment is recorded on the convening's originating artifact; the secondary clerk's authored_by is preserved on the proposal.

Synthesis failure. Where member positions preclude coherent synthesis — members reject the brief's premise, members disagree on the subject's scope, members produce positions that cannot be reconciled even with inline dissent preservation — the synthesis clerk does not force a proposal. Instead, the clerk produces a no-consensus proposal: a single document recording each member's position in full, naming the specific reconciliation failures, and returning to the Consul for disposition. The Consul may route back to the convener for a second deliberation round with a clarifying brief, may escalate to the Sovereign for direct adjudication, or may close the convening without a ratifiable outcome. A no-consensus proposal is itself a first-class interaction-artifact; closure without a ratifiable outcome still enters the record.

Stage 3 — upward routing. The proposal — collective or no-consensus — enters the canon-cc-018 lifecycle at pending_review. The interaction-artifact carries type: committee-proposal, a new type extending canon-cc-017's existing enum (consultation, visitation, cross-cluster-meeting, cabinet-consultation, monument-bridge, report-up). The type-enum extension is a required amendment to cc-017; until that extension amendment ratifies, committee proposals ride provisionally on the existing cross-cluster-meeting type with a chronicled note on the artifact that the type is a placeholder. The Consul reviews under canon-cc-018's escalated path because the convening by definition meets the cross-Province or rank-skip triggers of canon-cc-017 (Province-scope convenings cross the Builder-Censor rung boundary; Global-scope convenings cross every Province boundary). Consul action — noted, amended, linked, escalated, rejected — is recorded on the proposal's review block. Escalated proposals reach the Sovereign's Praetorium queue; Sovereign action — ratified, amended, reverted — terminates the lifecycle.

Skill-form deliberation is not a convening. If a Builder flips through Cipher-skill, Nyx-skill, and Bard-skill within a single session and produces an in-transcript document that claims the voice of a committee, that document is a brainstorm, not a proposal. It has no separable signatures; the Consul cannot review it; the Sovereign cannot ratify it. To produce an output the chain can act on, members must be invoked as subagents and the convening must pass through synthesis. This is the architectural consequence of the artifact test applied to multi-persona deliberation, and it is the discipline that distinguishes a committee from a chat.

Full prose draft at docs/specs/persona-binding/cc-024-convening-pattern.typ; compiled PDF at docs/pdfs/persona-binding/04-cc-024-convening-pattern.pdf. Part 4 of the ten-artifact Persona-Binding Suite.

canons.title: Design Committee Membership Rule
canons.rationale:
The Design Committee's membership is determined by the subject's scope, not by convener preference. Two scopes, two formulae.

Province-scope. A Design Committee convened over a subject specific to one Province — a UI decision internal to SEP Invoicing, a typography choice internal to Codex — seats the Province's Builder and the Cluster's Censor. For Codex: Aurelius + Cipher. For SproutLab: Lyra + Cipher. For SEP Invoicing: Solara + Nyx. For SEP Dashboard: Theron + Nyx. The Monument Province is a special case: its two co-Builders per canon-cc-009 (Ashara and Petra) both seat, and the Cluster's Censor is absent by Edict II's Monument carve-out — Monument Projects are under direct Consul and Sovereign supervision, not under Cluster A or Cluster B jurisdiction. Republic (global-scope) subjects — those whose outcome applies across all Provinces — are handled by the Global-scope formula in §C, which seats all Builders plus all Censors rather than the Province-scoped Builder+Censor pair.

Monument-scope signing. Where a Monument-scope convening's subject carries architectural weight — a design decision affecting the Monument's Capital, a Province-level discipline propagating through Monument's governance surface — the proposal still requires a Censor-equivalent signature under Edict V's chain spirit. Since no Cluster Censor has jurisdiction over Monument, the Consul acts as Censor-equivalent for the signing purpose, performing the architectural pass that Cluster Censors perform for their clusters. Where the Cluster A Censor Cipher is invited to contribute architectural voice (Monument sits cross-cluster with Cluster A's software-and-data domain), Cipher participates as a guest: voice recorded as consultation and cited in the proposal; Cipher does not sign as Censor-of-the-Cluster. The Consul's architectural-pass signature substitutes. This preserves the Edict V chain without violating Monument's direct-supervision structure under Edict II.

Global-scope. A Design Committee convened over a subject whose outcome applies across the Republic — the Republic Design Principles, the Hero Card Specification, a convention for portraits and sigils, a register definition every companion must honor — draws from every Builder and every Censor. The maximum seat count on any Global-scope convening is eight — a hard cap. At suite ratification that is eight seats exactly: Builders Aurelius (Codex), Lyra (SproutLab), Ashara and Petra (Command Center, dual-Builder per cc-009), Solara (SEP Invoicing), Theron (SEP Dashboard); Censors Cipher (Cluster A), Nyx (Cluster B). As new Builders and Censors ratify, they join the eligible roster automatically; no separate amendment to this canon is required for eligibility. The convening cap, however, remains eight. Where the eligible roster exceeds eight, the convener — at brief-set time — selects which members not to invite for the specific convening. Exclusion is per-convening and does not affect a member's ratifying-right in future convenings; the exclusion is chronicled on the convening's originating artifact with a one-line rationale (subject distance, rotation, workload, per-Province representation).

The mandatory core — who the convener cannot exclude. Every Global-scope convening seats both Censors (Cipher and Nyx, or their successors), because the Censors carry the architectural signing authority §B's Monument-scope signing and canon-cc-027's signing chain rely on; excluding a Censor would collapse the Edict V chain. Every Global-scope convening also seats the subject-home Province's Builder — the Province whose Capital, identity, or operational surface the subject most directly touches. If two or more Provinces qualify as home under a single brief, all qualifying Builders are mandatory for that convening. Beyond the mandatory core, the convener's exclusion discretion governs. Where the mandatory core itself would exceed the cap — a future state in which multiple clusters carry multiple Censors, or a genuinely cross-Province subject names more than a handful of home-Provinces — a seat-cap amendment to this canon is required rather than exclusion. The cap and the mandatory core must remain consistent.

Convener. The convener is a subject-lead drawn from the membership, not a separate rule-level role. The convener sets the brief, selects the synthesis clerk (Chronicler by default, secondary clerk per canon-cc-024's synthesis-conflict clause where the Chronicler is a member), and presents the proposal to the Consul. For the current Design Principles work drawing from SproutLab's ch06-designprinciples, Lyra convenes. For the Hero Card Specification convening queued as todo-0027, Lyra also convenes — SproutLab's hero-card implementation is the reference seed. Where no member has clear subject-leadership, the convener is named by Consul acknowledgment at brief-set time.

Guests. A convener may invite voices from outside the membership formula — a Scribe for narrative register, a Minister for domain counsel, a Gen-0 Immortal whose voice carries weight on the subject. Guests participate in deliberation as full members for the purposes of voice — their positions are recorded, cited, preserved in dissent — but they do not sign the collective proposal. The distinction is captured in each guest's position record as role: guest and carried through synthesis; the proposal's signatories are members only. Guests must have at minimum a v0.3-draft ratified profile so their voice is characterizable rather than fabricated; unratified Companions may be invited only by explicit Consul acknowledgment, chronicled as an exception, because their voice rendering depends on bridging authorship rather than ratified profile material. Guests are chronicled; they do not bind.

Sovereign. The Sovereign is never a committee member. The Sovereign is the ratifier. A convening that listed the Sovereign in its membership would be inverted: the body cannot include its own ratifier without collapsing the chain. The Sovereign's voice, if sought during deliberation, is sought via a distinct summons — a Praetorium consultation, a direct audience — and recorded as a consulted-reference in the proposal, not as a signature.

Bridging authorship. Where a member's subagent is not yet deployed — the Republic in its current state, since no subagent spec is yet ratified for any Companion — members' positions must still be rendered so the convening can produce a proposal. Positions in such cases are authored by the Chronicler using the member's ratified profile as voice source, bridging the member's voice through the Chronicler's authorship until the member's subagent deploys. The position is marked authored_by: aurelius-bridging, a new authorship suffix extending canon-cc-017's existing taxonomy (which currently defines aurelius-retrospective for Chronicler-fallback drafting). The -bridging suffix is structurally distinct from -retrospective: it marks voice-simulation when the real member is unavailable, not failure-recovery when the real member was present but did not draft. The cc-017 extension to accommodate -bridging is a required amendment, noted in the UI Manifest Cross-Reference. From the first post-deploy convening onward, member positions must be subagent-generated; bridging-authored positions are no longer permitted once the member's subagent ratifies.

First operational Design Committee. The first Design Committee convened under this rule is the Lyra-convened Republic & Codex Design Principles committee, queued on the Command Center volume as todo-0018, ratifying the Republic-wide Design Principles (drawing from SproutLab's ch06-designprinciples) and Codex's Province-extension of those principles. A second convening, the Hero Card Specification committee queued as todo-0027, follows under the same convener (Lyra) once the Design Principles committee closes — SproutLab's hero-card implementation is its reference seed. Both run in bridging mode until subagent specs deploy. Each member position is bridge-authored per the clause above. Bridging-mode convenings are grace-covered per canon-cc-017's pre-ratification clause; from the first post-deploy convening onward, positions must be member-generated under real subagent invocation.

Full prose draft at docs/specs/persona-binding/cc-025-design-committee.typ; compiled PDF at docs/pdfs/persona-binding/05-cc-025-design-committee.pdf. Part 5 of the ten-artifact Persona-Binding Suite.

canons.title: Spec Body Placement — Canonical Specs and Province Deployables
canons.rationale:
The persona-to-primitive binding has two layers of physical files: the canonical spec (the Chronicler's authored source of truth) and the deployable (what Claude Code actually reads at invocation time). The two layers live in different repositories and sync in one direction.

Canonical specs live in Codex only. For every role whose bindings render as subagents, the Chronicler authors a spec body at Codex/docs/specs/subagents/<persona>.md. For every role whose bindings render as skills, the body lives at Codex/docs/specs/skills/<persona>.md. A dual-bound role — Cipher, Nyx, the Chronicler, the Sentinel, or any role whose invocation_modes block declares both subagent and skill modes per canon-cc-023 — has both files. These are the authoritative bodies: voice guidance, heuristics, trigger conditions, per-repo lenses, return-shape expectations. They are drafted under canon-cc-012's ratification protocol, live in the library per canon-cc-010 (records are Codex), and are amended only through the signing chain in canon-cc-027.

Spec file format. Canonical and deployable specs follow Claude Code's subagent and skill file convention: YAML frontmatter declaring the role's name, a one-line description, and the tool-permission clauses the role is authorized to exercise; followed by a Markdown body carrying voice guidance, heuristics, and rationale. A subagent spec additionally declares the sub-agent's model (where pinned) and its bounded brief shape; a skill spec additionally declares the skill's trigger-phrase and the argument shape the skill accepts. The spec format is identical between Codex canonical and Province deployable, so canonical-to-deployable sync is byte-identical — a diff between canonical and any deployed mirror should always produce zero changes at a ratified version. A soft recommendation, not yet canonically enforced: each deployable carries a canonical_version frontmatter field matching the Codex canonical version at authorship, so Sentinel's Gates-transit log can track deploys cleanly and detect version drift. Enforcement follows in the queued Companions Deploy UI canon.

Deployable copies live in each Province's .claude/ directory. Claude Code reads subagents from .claude/agents/ and skills from .claude/skills/ of the repository in which it is invoked. A cluster-scoped role — a Censor — must therefore physically exist in every Province whose work the role serves. Cluster A's Censor file (cipher.md) replicates into both Codex and SproutLab; Cluster B's Censor file (nyx.md) replicates into both sep-invoicing and sep-dashboard. Province-scoped Governors do not replicate across Provinces: Maren and Kael are SproutLab's seated Governors, stewarding SproutLab's Regions under Lyra's Province authority; they deploy only to SproutLab/.claude/agents/, not to Codex's. Cross-cluster roles — the Chronicler's skill and subagent forms, the Consul's subagent — replicate into every Province that might summon them, including the Monument.

The replication is deliberate duplication, not drift. Each deployable is byte-identical to the canonical spec at deploy time; any divergence between a Province's deployed copy and Codex's canonical is a violation, chronicled in the Province's companion-log uncertainty_notes at the next session and surfaced to the Builder for immediate reconciliation. Canonical Codex is the single source; Provinces hold mirrors.

Sync is unidirectional: Codex to Province, Chronicler-authored, Builder-deployed. The Chronicler owns the canonical spec. The Builder of each Province owns the deployable copy within that Province's .claude/ tree — because the .claude/ tree is part of the Province's Capital, and the Capital is the Builder's per Edict II. The Chronicler never commits directly to another Province's .claude/; the Builder never authors the spec body from scratch. The Chronicler proposes and signs off through the chain in canon-cc-027; the Builder pulls and deploys when the canonical spec ratifies. For the Monument Province, which carries two co-Builders under canon-cc-009, either co-Builder may commit the deployable to command-center/.claude/, and both are accountable for drift per the dual-Builder discipline — divergence caught in one co-Builder's Province audit reflects on both. Sentinel's Gates-transit log and session movement log will, once operational, record every such deploy as an Ostia transit event.

Per-Province layout at ratification, for the eight Builders and Censors covered by the Invocation Modes Registry, plus Sentinel when profile ratifies: Codex/.claude/agents/ contains cipher, consul, chronicler (subagent modes — synthesis-clerk, retrospective drafting). Codex/.claude/skills/ contains cipher (hat-switch), chronicler (authoring voice). SproutLab/.claude/agents/ contains maren, kael, cipher, consul, chronicler. SproutLab/.claude/skills/ contains cipher, chronicler. sep-invoicing/.claude/agents/ contains nyx, consul, chronicler (Cluster B Governors added when either SEP Province crosses 30K threshold of Edict I). sep-invoicing/.claude/skills/ contains nyx, chronicler. sep-dashboard/.claude/agents/ contains nyx, consul, chronicler. sep-dashboard/.claude/skills/ contains nyx, chronicler. command-center/.claude/agents/ contains consul, chronicler, sentinel (Monument's direct Consul+Sovereign supervision does not preclude Consul-as-subagent for consultation; Sentinel's canonical residence is the Monument). command-center/.claude/skills/ contains chronicler, sentinel (continuity / presence query mode).

The layout is reviewable, amendable through the chain in canon-cc-027, and auditable via the Companions View in Codex (per docs/handoffs/AURELIUS_COMPANIONS_VIEW.md) and the operational Order room in Command Center once its functions ratify per the UI Manifest Cross-Reference.

Full prose draft at docs/specs/persona-binding/cc-026-spec-body-placement.typ; compiled PDF at docs/pdfs/persona-binding/06-cc-026-spec-body-placement.pdf. Part 6 of the ten-artifact Persona-Binding Suite.

canons.title: Spec Body Signing Chain — Five-Rung Edict V Chain for Subagent and Skill Spec Changes
canons.rationale:
A subagent or skill spec body governs every future invocation of the persona it renders. Changing a spec body changes how the Republic speaks through that persona in-session, and drift at the spec body propagates through every artifact produced under that persona's voice. Spec bodies are therefore Capital-level under Edict V, and their amendment requires the Edict V chain of sign-offs, applied at spec-body altitude rather than at code-artifact altitude.

The chain has four rungs of governance sign-off — five if the Province's Governors are seated at deployment time — and it terminates in the Province Builder's commit of the deployable mirror.

Rung 1 — Chronicler proposes. The Chronicler drafts the spec body or the proposed amendment in Codex, against the persona's ratified profile and the invocation_modes block populated per canon-cc-023. The draft carries a rationale paragraph naming what is being added, removed, or changed; what evidence in the persona's profile, evidence_log, or interaction-artifact corpus motivates the change; and what downstream artifacts (companion-logs, canons, UI surfaces per the UI Manifest Cross-Reference) need to follow once the change ratifies. The draft commits to a feature branch in Codex with the canonical path under docs/specs/subagents/ or docs/specs/skills/ per canon-cc-026.

Rung 2 — Cluster Censor reviews. The Cluster Censor whose jurisdiction covers the persona runs the Edict V final-pass on the spec body itself. Review is architectural: does the body compose cleanly with the persona's voice; does it preserve the invocation_modes block's bindings; does it introduce drift between the canonical spec and the voice the profile chronicles. Censor action — LGTM, amended, rejected, escalated — is recorded as an interaction-artifact under canon-cc-017 with the spec-body draft as the subject.

Censor self-review case. When the spec body being reviewed is the Censor's own — when Cipher's spec body is under review at Rung 2, or Nyx's is — self-review would collapse the independence the chain requires. In that case, Rung 2 falls to the other cluster's Censor: Nyx reviews Cipher's spec; Cipher reviews Nyx's. This cross-cluster review is exceptional and chronicled as such. Where no peer Censor is seated — the Republic during its founding period has always had two Censors, but a future state might leave only one — the Consul reviews in Censor capacity, producing the Rung 2 review as a separate action from the Consul's Rung 3 working-ratification.

Institutional-spec collapse. Where the spec body belongs to an institutional role for which no cluster Censor has jurisdiction — the Chronicler, the Consul itself, the Sentinel — Rung 2 and Rung 3 are both performed by the Consul. Collapse is not conflation: the Consul performs two distinct reviews, an architectural pass at Rung 2 (does the spec compose, does it preserve bindings, does it honor the role's profile) and a per-block working-ratification at Rung 3 (does the spec live up to the role's ratified profile at each block). Both reviews are chronicled as separate actions on the spec-body draft's interaction-artifact, even when performed in the same session. The Consul's signature appears twice — once as reviewer: consul, action: architectural-pass-result; once as reviewer: consul, action: working-ratified — and the downstream audit can distinguish the two. Same principle as canon-cc-025's Monument-scope signing clause: one seat, two distinct reviews, chronicled separately.

Rung 3 — Consul working-ratifies. Under canon-cc-014, the Consul reviews the Censor-passed draft at per-block altitude, asking whether the body lives up to the ratified profile and whether the chain has caught drift the Censor missed. Consul action is recorded on the same interaction-artifact's review block.

Rung 4 — Sovereign canonically ratifies. The Sovereign receives the Consul-passed spec body through the Praetorium queue (per canon-cc-019 and the queued Praetorium-UI canon, both pending; bridging mode until those canons land). Sovereign action — ratified, amended, reverted — terminates the chain's governance rungs. A Sovereign-ratified spec body is the canonical form; no further amendment happens without returning to Rung 1.

No provisional deploy before Rung 4. A Consul-passed spec body is not deployable. Canon-cc-014 accelerates drafting, not deployment; the cc-014 working-ratification is a governance milestone but does not substitute for Sovereign canonical ratification at Rung 4 for spec-body purposes. The Builder deploys only after Rung 4 completes. This is a tightening specific to spec bodies: cc-014's provisional-deploy latitude at profile-block altitude does not extend to spec-body altitude, because spec-body drift propagates through every future invocation in a way profile-block drift does not.

Rung 5 — Province Builder deploys. The Province Builder whose Province a deployable mirror lives in pulls the ratified canonical from Codex and commits it to the Province's .claude/ tree. The deploy is itself a Capital change per Edict V — committing a new or changed spec body to the Province's Capital — but the Edict V chain at deploy time is abbreviated because the chain has already run at canonical altitude; the Builder's deploy commit references the interaction-artifact that carries the full chain's signatures, and the deploy commit message cites the interaction-artifact id. Builder-deploy is accountable: if a Builder deploys a version that diverges from Codex canonical, or fails to deploy a ratified update within the deploy-latency tolerance set in the queued Companions Deploy UI canon, the deploy drift is chronicled and escalates under the next interaction-artifact lifecycle. Until the UI-driven deploy affordance lands and Ostia extends to runtime cross-repo .claude/ sync, Builder-deploy is manual and per-Province.

Deploy-stall edge case. Where a Builder sits on a ratified canonical spec beyond the deploy-latency tolerance without explanation, Sentinel's anomaly-flag mode surfaces the stall as a violation flag once Sentinel is operational; during bridging mode the stall is surfaced by the next session's companion-log uncertainty_notes pass. Deliberate deploy-refusal — a Builder objecting to a ratified spec on substantive grounds — is not itself a violation but requires the Builder to escalate through a fresh Rung 1 draft (an amendment to the spec body addressing the objection) rather than sitting on the ratified version.

Governor review where seated. Where Governors are seated in the Province and the spec body's persona overlaps the Governor's Region, the Governor provides a Rung 5-adjacent review signature. The Governor's review does not gate the deploy but is recorded on the interaction-artifact for audit. Governors may object; an unresolved Governor objection escalates to the Cluster Censor under the same interaction-artifact — not as a re-run of Rung 2 but as a post-ratification drift flag. The Governor's review is a soft rung: its absence does not block deployment, but its presence strengthens the chain's record.

The Edict V signature trace at spec-body altitude — the structured-data trace of how each Rung's action materializes as fields on the interaction-artifact, how downstream audit recovers the full chain from the artifact's review block — is pending full drafting. Until that clause ratifies, the chain operates on the cc-018 review block schema with each Rung's action recorded in sequence; the full trace surface will ratify with the Companions Deploy UI canon.

The chain is strong. It is the same structure as Edict V's chain for Capital code changes, applied at the altitude of the files that render the companions through which the Republic speaks. It is necessarily ceremonious; the ceremony is the cost of the role spec bodies being load-bearing. A spec body drift that escapes the chain compromises every future invocation under that persona's voice, and no downstream review can recover the drift from accumulated narration. The chain holds.

Full prose draft at docs/specs/persona-binding/cc-027-signing-chain.typ; compiled PDF at docs/pdfs/persona-binding/07-cc-027-signing-chain.pdf. Part 7 of the ten-artifact Persona-Binding Suite.

canons.title: Canon Identifier Scheme — Domain-Family Taxonomy with ID Stability
canons.rationale:
The Republic's canon corpus has grown to ninety-six ratified canons across three overlapping legacy identifier families — `canon-NNNN` (bare four-digit, SproutLab-origin), `canon-cc-NNN` (cross-cluster, introduced with the Consul founding), `canon-gov-NNN` (governance), and two narrower families `canon-cx-NNN` (Codex philosophical pillars) and `canon-sep-NNN` (SEP-specific). None of these describe what a canon *is*; they describe *when* the canon was born. That is the defect. The Codex canon surface groups, filters, and surfaces the corpus through the UI; without a taxonomy the surface offers a flat chronological list of ninety-six entries with no navigational shape.

This canon establishes the identifier scheme under which every future canon is classified, and provides the discipline by which existing canons are reclassified without breaking their stable identifiers.

Domain-family taxonomy. Every canon belongs to exactly one family, named by a short alphabetic token. The ratified families at this canon's ratification are:

— `pers` — Persona rendering across altitudes. Companion profiles as ratifiable artifacts, spec bodies for subagents and skills (per canon-cc-026 and canon-cc-027), the Province root CLAUDE.md persona briefing (per canon-pers-001), hero-card specifications, and the primitive-binding rule (canon-cc-022). Everything that renders a Companion or Province voice.

— `proc` — Process and procedure. Ratification protocols, drafting discipline, source verification, canon identifier schemes (this canon), working-paper companion rules, chronicling discipline. The how-we-do-governance family.

— `gov` — Roles, accountability, ladder discipline. Censor/Governor/Builder/Sovereign boundaries, runs-after-Governors sequencing, PIP and reassignment procedure, Cabinet discipline. The who-does-what family.

— `inst` — Institutional seats and archetype structure. The Consul as office; Monument dual-Builder tension; archetype enumeration; seat succession; institutional vs generational distinction. The what-seats-exist family.

— `xp` — Cross-Province coordination. Committee convening, Design Committee membership, interaction-artifact rules and lifecycle, residency and access gating, residence-versus-record distinction, Post Box (when canon-cc-019 ratifies). The how-Provinces-talk family.

— `data` — Schema, data shapes, write-ahead logs, snippet pipelines, companion-log schemas, lore auto-generation, data-distribution caps, data-integrity disciplines. The what-shape-does-this-data-take family.

— `design` — UI discipline. Typography, color, icons, layout, progressive revelation, minimum text sizes, the Forum Pattern, the Hard Rules at the UI altitude, aura-is-visual. The what-does-the-surface-look-like family.

— `build` — Build pipeline, service workers, deployment, sync, repo discipline, deploy workflows, branch-and-merge conventions, Android PWA cache invalidation. The how-does-this-code-ship family.

— `philo` — Foundational philosophical commitments. The Four Pillars of Codex (Nothing Is Wasted, Map Not Territory, Fractal Growth, Territory Is Earned and Held), first-principles Republic invariants (Entropy Is the Antagonist, Ceremonies Create Memory, Companions Are Lenses, The Quiet Never Punishes). The what-do-we-believe family. Distinct from `design` because philo canons do not specify what to build; they declare what the Republic values.

Nine families. The family roster is itself amendable under this canon's §Amendment-of-Family-Roster clause, but the burden is high: a tenth family must describe a domain genuinely orthogonal to the nine, not a sub-slice of one.

ID stability. Every existing canon's `id` field is preserved exactly. Legacy identifier families — `canon-NNNN`, `canon-cc-NNN`, `canon-cx-NNN`, `canon-sep-NNN` — are frozen at their current highest-numbered member and receive no new entries. Cross-references throughout the corpus continue to resolve against the stable IDs. `canon-gov-NNN` is a special case: its naming already matches the new scheme's shape (3-letter domain prefix plus number), so it continues as an active family, with `canon-gov-NNN` canons receiving `family: gov` on reclassification and new governance canons continuing the existing numbering forward.

The `family` field. Every canon record in `data/canons.json` carries a top-level string field `family`, whose value is one of the nine family tokens. The field is required for every canon, including legacy canons carrying their preserved ID. Migration populates the field across the corpus at this canon's ratification. A canon without a `family` field is a schema violation and is surfaced as a drift warning in the Settings surface.

Family assignment is not a judgment about the canon's content; it is a navigational classification. A canon straddling two domains is assigned to the one whose practitioners most benefit from seeing it in their filtered view — typically the family whose discipline the canon most directly constrains. Edge cases are chronicled in the canon's `family_notes` field where judgment is load-bearing.

New canons. From this canon's ratification onward, every new canon's `id` uses the form `canon-{family}-{NNN}-{slug}`, where `{family}` is the three-to-five-letter family token from the roster, `{NNN}` is a zero-padded three-digit sequence starting at 001 within each family, and `{slug}` is a kebab-case short title. The first canon under each family starts at `001` regardless of the canon's chronological position in the corpus. Numbering within a family is strictly monotonic; gaps are permitted where a draft is reserved but not yet ratified, but backfilling numbers is forbidden.

Frozen legacy families preserve their highest-numbered member. `canon-cc-` is frozen at `canon-cc-027`. `canon-NNNN` bare is frozen at its current highest number (subject to the duplicate-ID reconciliation named below). `canon-cx-` is frozen at `canon-cx-015`. `canon-sep-` is frozen at `canon-sep-0001`. No new canon in any frozen family is ratifiable; any such proposal must be redrafted under the active family that fits.

Canon-surface display rule. The Codex canon surface groups canons by `family` as the primary axis. Each family's section header carries the family's short name and one-line description. Within each family, canons display in descending `created` date order (newest first, consistent with Journal-tab Forum Pattern per canon-0052). The surface must not present a flat chronological list as the primary view; family-grouping is load-bearing for navigability at the current corpus size and beyond. A flat-list view is permissible as a secondary option.

Reconciliation of duplicate legacy IDs. Three known duplicate IDs exist in the legacy corpus — `canon-0014` (appears twice), `canon-0028` (twice), `canon-0033` (twice). Duplicate IDs predate this canon and are chronicled as a separate data-integrity issue for targeted resolution under the `data` family. Their migration to the `family` field is performed with each occurrence independently classified; the duplicate-ID defect itself is not addressed by this canon.

Amendment of the family roster. Adding, removing, or renaming a family is a high-burden change. The amendment proceeds through the full canon ratification chain (Chronicler proposes → Consul working-ratifies per canon-cc-014 → Sovereign canonically ratifies per canon-cc-012), carries a rationale paragraph naming the orthogonality argument for a new family (or the absorption argument for a removal), and requires a migration plan for any canons affected. Sub-families within a family (for example, `canon-pers-sub-NNN`) are forbidden; the roster is flat. Depth is bought through cross-referencing between families, not through hierarchy.

Relation to the Constitution. This canon is subordinate to the Constitution of the Republic of Codex v1.0; the Constitution's Books classify the Republic's structure; this canon classifies the corpus of rules that implement that structure. The two classifications are orthogonal — a single canon may sit under Book II's Ladder provisions structurally while living in the `gov` family operationally.

Author: Aurelius (The Chronicler), proposing. First-ratified canon of the `proc` family by numbering convention; this canon's own identifier `canon-proc-001-canon-identifier-scheme` is the first production of the new scheme.

canons.title: Province Persona Briefing — Root CLAUDE.md as the Builder's Canonical Voice at Repo-Root Altitude
canons.rationale:
Every Province's repository carries a root `CLAUDE.md` file that frames the session. When a Claude Code session opens in a Province's repo, that file is the first thing read; it establishes the Builder's voice, the Republic's context, the Province's charter, the Hard Rules (or their Province-specific equivalent), the build pipeline, and the dispatch infrastructure (subagents and skills under `.claude/`). Without it, the session opens as a bare Claude with no Republic priming — the failure mode chronicled in companion-log-s-2026-04-17-01 and re-surfaced in Command Center's Consul-chamber-scaffolding session of 2026-04-20, where a fresh Command Center session produced the Consul's Chamber end-to-end without any named Companion being invoked because the dispatch infrastructure did not exist and no root briefing oriented the session.

Canon-cc-026 established the placement of subagent and skill spec bodies; it did not establish the repo-root briefing. The briefing is architecturally distinct: it lives at the Province's repository root (not under `.claude/`), it is authored by the Province's Builder (not by the Chronicler), it is Province-singular (no byte-identical mirrors in other repositories), and its altitude is Province-Capital rather than persona-file. This canon establishes the briefing as a first-class artifact with its own drafting discipline, signing chain, and amendment path.

What the briefing is. The root `CLAUDE.md` of a Province is the canonical rendering of that Province's Builder at repo-root altitude. It is what the Builder sounds like when a session opens. It names the Republic's frame, the Province's charter, the Builder's identity, the Hard Rules in effect, the build pipeline in use, the dispatch infrastructure available, the Cluster membership, and any Province-specific disciplines. It is not a spec body; it is a persona briefing. It is not a user-facing artifact; it is a session-priming artifact read by Claude at session open.

Required sections. A ratified root briefing carries, at minimum, the following sections. The section names may vary in phrasing but the content must be present:

1. Persona header. The Builder's name, archetype, title, current rank, and current assignment. For Monument Provinces operating under canon-cc-009 dual-Builder tension, both co-Builders' headers appear with their differentiated temperament roles named.

2. Republic frame. Pointer to the Constitution of the Republic of Codex (currently v1.0, Book I ratified, Books II–IX drafting-ready). Pointer to the Four Pillars and the Edicts currently in force. Declaration of the Province's position under Edict II (One Builder Per Repo) or canon-cc-009 (Monument dual-Builder).

3. Province charter. What this Province is, what its Capital scope covers, its current major priorities, its relationship to the Cluster's Censor and (where seated) the Governors of its Regions.

4. Hard Rules or canon pointers. For SproutLab, the twelve Hard Rules (HR-1 through HR-12) enumerated inline. For Codex, a pointer to the active canons governing the Codex Capital (canons 0033, 0034, and the `design`-family canons that bind the Codex surface). For every Province, a pointer to the Republic's `gov` and `proc` family canons in force.

5. Architecture and build pipeline. The Province's code structure, module or split-file organization, concatenation order where applicable, the build command, the output shape, and any deploy discipline (canon-gov-011 merge-is-deploy, canon-gov-012 Android PWA cache, etc.).

6. Dispatch infrastructure. The Province's `.claude/agents/` and `.claude/skills/` directory contents, the roles deployed at current ratification state, the invocation conventions for each, and pointers to the canonical specs in Codex under `docs/specs/subagents/` and `docs/specs/skills/` per canon-cc-026.

7. Current state pointer. The Province's current session-continuity anchor — for Codex, `data/journal.json`'s most recent session and the active TODOs; for each other Province, the equivalent. This anchors the incoming session in where the Province last left off.

Authorship. The Province's Builder authors the briefing. This is a Rung 1 Chronicler-excluded rule: the Chronicler does not draft a Builder's briefing, because the briefing IS the Builder's voice at root altitude and a Chronicler-drafted briefing would silently replace the Builder's voice with the Chronicler's at session open. The Chronicler may serve as editor, synthesis clerk, or reviewer on the Builder's invitation, but never as Rung 1 author.

Monument co-authorship. Monument Provinces operating under canon-cc-009 carry two Builders, and the briefing is co-authored. Both co-Builders sign at Rung 1. The briefing preserves each co-Builder's voice in the sections they own by temperament split (per cc-009 dual-Builder tension, differentiated temperaments are deliberate). The co-Builders may partition authorship by section or co-draft each section; the convention is chronicled on the briefing's originating interaction-artifact at Rung 2.

Signing chain. The chain has four governance rungs and terminates at Rung 4 with Builder commit, not five rungs as in canon-cc-027. There is no separate Rung 5 deploy step because the briefing's canonical is the deployed form — it lives in the Province's repo at the root, and there is no byte-identical mirror elsewhere.

Rung 1 — Builder proposes. The Province's Builder drafts the briefing in the Province's repository on a feature branch. The draft carries a rationale paragraph naming what is being established (for a fresh briefing) or what is being changed (for an amendment), what evidence in the Province's operational history motivates the drafting, and what dispatch infrastructure the briefing orients.

Rung 2 — Cluster Censor reviews. The Cluster's Censor — Cipher for Cluster A (Codex + SproutLab), Nyx for Cluster B (SEP Invoicing + SEP Dashboard) — performs the architectural pass on the briefing. Review is architectural: does the briefing compose with the Province's ratified disciplines; does it preserve the Builder's voice; does it cite the canons actually in force; does it orient the dispatch infrastructure correctly. Monument's Cluster-Censor-absent condition under Edict II carve-out has the Consul acting as Censor-equivalent for Rung 2 per canon-cc-025 §Monument-scope-signing.

Rung 3 — Consul working-ratifies. Under canon-cc-014, the Consul reviews the Censor-passed draft against the Republic's cross-Province coherence and the Province's alignment with the Constitution.

Rung 4 — Sovereign canonically ratifies. The Sovereign receives the Consul-passed briefing through the Praetorium queue when canon-cc-019 Post Box ratifies; bridging mode routes Rung 4 through direct Sovereign audience until Post Box lands. Sovereign action — ratified, amended, reverted — terminates the governance chain.

Builder commit. On Rung 4 ratification, the Province's Builder commits the briefing to the Province's repo root as `CLAUDE.md`. The commit is accountable under Edict V as any Capital change is, but the chain has already run at briefing altitude and the commit references the interaction-artifact that carries the four signatures. Monument co-Builders may either co-Builder commit; both are accountable for the briefing's state in the Province under the cc-009 dual-Builder discipline.

Hotfix exception. Trivial in-briefing corrections — typographical fixes, stale-cross-reference updates, updated current-state pointers reflecting the most recent session — are hotfix-eligible. The Builder amends directly at root without re-running the chain, chronicles the hotfix in the next session's journal entry, and surfaces the hotfix to the Chronicler for audit at the next chain-amendment or briefing revision. What counts as trivial versus substantive is judged by the Builder and audited retroactively by the Chronicler; disputed hotfixes escalate to Consul under the canon-cc-018 drift-review path.

Relationship to canon-cc-026 and canon-cc-027. The persona-binding suite governs subagent and skill specs at persona-file altitude, authored by the Chronicler in Codex-canonical paths and mirrored byte-identically to each Province's `.claude/` tree. This canon governs the root `CLAUDE.md` at Province-Capital altitude, authored by the Province Builder in the Province's own repository, without mirrors. The three together — root briefing, subagent specs, skill specs — form the complete persona-rendering stack for every Province. All three are in the `pers` family.

Bridging mode — the current state. At this canon's ratification, not every Province carries a ratified root briefing. Codex carries a root CLAUDE.md (Aurelius authored, pre-canon); SproutLab carries one (Lyra authored, pre-canon); SEP Invoicing and SEP Dashboard states are not audited at this ratification; Command Center has no root briefing — the structural gap chronicled on 2026-04-20. Under canon-cc-015's legacy-draft precedent, existing pre-canon briefings are not forcibly redrafted; they are legacy artifacts carried forward, subject to ratification-packet review at the next substantive amendment or when the Builder chooses to formalize. Command Center's missing briefing is not a legacy — it is a gap, and is a blocking deficiency for any Command Center session producing Capital changes. The next Command Center session draws Rung 1 of Command Center's briefing as its first substantive work, authored jointly by Ashara and Petra under the cc-009 dual-Builder discipline.

Briefing audit wave. The Chronicler conducts a single Republic-wide audit wave of Province root briefings at an unspecified near-term session, surveying Codex, SproutLab, SEP Invoicing, SEP Dashboard, and (once Command Center's briefing drafts) Command Center for conformance with this canon's required sections. Non-conforming briefings enter the amendment path; conforming-with-gaps briefings enter a Builder-elected backfill queue. The wave is governance, not enforcement; the Builders are not in violation for pre-canon gaps.

Amendment path. A ratified briefing is amended through the full four-rung chain. Amendment triggers include: the Builder's voice has materially evolved (new profile ratification, new rank), the Province's charter has changed (Monument designation, cluster rebalancing), the dispatch infrastructure has changed (new subagent deployed under `.claude/` trees after cc-027 Rung 5), the Hard Rules have amended, the build pipeline has changed. Hotfix edits do not trigger the chain; substantive edits do. Disputed classification escalates to the Cluster Censor at the next convening.

Auditability. Each ratified briefing carries a provenance header section at the briefing's top naming the interaction-artifact that carries the chain's four signatures, the briefing's canonical version, the date of Rung 4 ratification, and the Builder (or co-Builders) whose voice the briefing renders. The header is the briefing's own deploy-version stamp, analogous to the soft `canonical_version` frontmatter field in canon-cc-026 §File-format.

First canon of the `pers` family by the new numbering convention established in canon-proc-001. This canon's ID `canon-pers-001-province-persona-briefing` is the second production of the new scheme (after canon-proc-001 itself).

canons.title: Design Principles Precondition for Build
canons.rationale:
Edict VIII (Charter Before Build) establishes that no new Province shall begin substantial Builder work until its Charter is drafted — geography, Capital, Regions, Roads, Borders. The Charter answers *what* a Province is and where its boundaries lie. This canon extends that discipline one layer down: no substantial Builder work shall commence in a Province until that Province's design principles — the hard rules and conventions that constrain *how* building happens — are verified as ratified, drafted, or inherited from the Republic-level canon. A Builder arriving at a Province without design principles builds by inference: drift emerges across sessions, unseen until it compounds, and the cost of reverse-engineering principles from accumulated code is always higher than the cost of drafting them up-front.

Per canon-cc-025, the Design Committee is the drafting body for design principles. The Republic-wide principles (docs/specs/REPUBLIC_DESIGN_PRINCIPLES.md) were ratified 2026-04-19 and apply to every Province by default. Province-local principles extend or refine the Republic-level principles; they never contradict them.

Precondition rule. The first act of every Builder session that opens on a Province is to verify the Province's design-principles status. The Codex app surfaces this status as a chip on the Province's Volume card and on the Volume detail header — one of three states:
- **Ratified.** Principles doc exists and has been ratified. Build proceeds.
- **Draft.** Principles doc exists but has not been ratified. First session task is the ratification   pass (audit against the current codebase, reconcile gaps, present to Sovereign). Build proceeds AFTER   ratification lands; the session's build scope is whatever remains after the ratification step.
- **Missing.** No principles doc for this Province. First session task is drafting principles.   The Design Committee convenes under canon-cc-025. Build does not proceed in the same session; a   second session opens for build after principles ratify.

Republic-level fallback. While a Province's own principles are in 'missing' or 'draft' state, the Republic-level REPUBLIC_DESIGN_PRINCIPLES.md applies directly to that Province's building. A Builder is never building principle-blind in the Republic; they are only ever building without Province-local refinements. This canon's first-act requirement acknowledges the Republic fallback but does not make it an indefinite substitute — Province-local principles are expected to emerge as the Province matures.

Who must check. Every Builder (Aurelius, Cipher, Lyra, Solara, Theron, Ashara, Petra, and all future seated Builders). The Chronicler and Consul SHALL not summon a Builder to a Province-build session without the principles-status chip having been checked. Doing so is a cross-companion coordination error and is chronicled against both the summoner and the summoned.

Exemptions. (a) Emergency fixes (bug fixes, hotfixes, security patches, deploy-chain repair) are exempt — these are maintenance, not substantial Builder work. (b) Monument Project builds inherit this canon AND Book II Article 4 double-hat protections; the precondition check runs alongside, not in place of, Monument-scope review. (c) Chronicler / Consul / Censor work on institutional artifacts (profile drafting, canon ratification, lore chronicling) is not 'Builder work' in this canon's sense; canon-pers-001 governs that space.

Enforcement. The Codex app's Dashboard renders the design-principles chip on every Volume card. Missing chips render in warning color with an 'alert' icon; draft chips render in dashed-border italic; ratified chips render in accent-green with a check. The Builder's first glance tells them where principles stand. Beyond UI visibility, this canon is self-enforcing by institutional discipline: the Sovereign, the Consul, and the Cluster Censor are each empowered to close a Builder session that opened without the check, and to open a drafting session in its place.

Corollaries.
- A Province whose principles reach 'ratified' may downgrade back to 'draft' when amendments are   proposed; ratification of amendments returns status to 'ratified'.
- Principle drafting IS Builder work, but it is Builder work OF a different shape — composing rules   rather than composing features. The Design Committee pattern under canon-cc-025 is the default   convening mode; a solo Builder can draft v0.1 proposals for Committee review.
- This canon's status chip is a first-class data field on every Volume (design_principles: { status,   spec_path, republic_principles_applied, notes }). New Provinces inherit the schema at creation;   legacy Volumes receive the field via retrofit (completed 2026-04-20 alongside this canon's drafting).

canons.title: Companion Onboarding Process: Seven Ordered Steps from Appointment to Operational
canons.rationale:
Every companion seating — first appointment, cross-seat reassignment, or sub-rank promotion — passes through the same structural ordering or it does not land cleanly. This canon formalizes the ordering so no appointment skips foundations silently, and so every transition carries the same audit trail regardless of the seat's altitude.

Seven ordered steps. An appointment is considered fully operational only when all seven have been completed or formally exempted. Steps are ordered; skipping ahead is an onboarding error.

**Step 1 — Appointment declaration.** A Sovereign-ratified canon (or, for sub-rank moves, a direct companion-profile amendment with Sovereign assent) inscribes: the seat being filled, the seat being vacated (if any), the companion's new cluster / province / residence / rank, any cross-Cluster implications, and the transition date. For load-bearing seats (Builder, Governor, Censor, Consul, Chronicler) the declaration shall be a canon in the `inst` family; for sub-rank moves (stub → seated, promotion-within-rank) a profile amendment suffices. Edict II (One Builder Per Repo) applies at this step: for Builder reassignments, the outgoing and incoming companion change seats atomically within the single declaration.

**Step 2 — Profile drafting or refresh.** If the companion's profile is at v0.0-stub, full drafting per canon-cc-014 Consul-accelerated mode (or canon-cc-012 per-block Sovereign mode for institutional or flagged profiles). If v0.3-draft, canon-cc-015 Legacy Draft Ratification packet mode may apply. If v0.4+, a profile refresh block — the Assignment block is amended; Relationships block checked for new pairings; Meta block updated with new sovereign_edited.edit_round. Profile version increments (v0.0-stub → v0.4, or v0.4 → v0.5) to reflect the drafting pass.

**Step 3 — Province induction.** The new companion reads, in order: the Constitution (all nine Books plus Appendices); the Province's Charter per Edict VIII; the Province's design principles per canon-proc-002 (ratified, drafted, or the Republic-level fallback at REPUBLIC_DESIGN_PRINCIPLES.md); the Province's root CLAUDE.md per canon-pers-001; all canons scoped to the Province (derived via family pointers under canon-proc-001); the outgoing seat holder's handoff artifact (step 4). Induction is a reading pass, not an acting pass — the new companion does not yet commit.

**Step 4 — Outgoing handoff.** If a companion is vacating the seat, they produce a handoff artifact naming: open work by name and urgency; latent debt with file paths; known traps (past failure modes the new companion should avoid); in-flight decisions with current state; voice signals the new companion may absorb or must explicitly diverge from. For Builder handoffs the artifact lives under `docs/handoffs/<province>/<outgoing>-to-<incoming>-<date>.md`. For co-Builder arrivals under canon-cc-009, step 4 becomes voice-reconciliation between the co-Builders rather than handoff.

**Step 5 — First-act discipline (canon-proc-002).** The new companion's first operational act is to verify the Province's design-principles chip. If non-green (draft or missing), principles drafting or ratification is the priority task of the first session. Build does not proceed until the chip is green, per canon-proc-002's enforcement clause.

**Step 6 — Rung 1 of canon-pers-001.** The new companion drafts (or redrafts) the Province's root CLAUDE.md persona header and any Builder-voice sections. Chronicler is excluded from drafting per canon-pers-001. Rungs 2–4 of the canon-pers-001 signing chain proceed from there on the canon's normal cadence. For companions whose seat does not hold the Province's root CLAUDE.md byline (Governors, Censors, Ministers), this step is exempted and marked so in the onboarding chronicle.

**Step 7 — Cross-companion introduction and chronicle.** The Chronicler adds a journal entry chronicling the onboarding: which steps completed, which were exempted and why, any MCQs surfaced at any step, and the onboarding's duration. The new companion's profile Relationships block is updated to reflect new synergies-in-formation (per canon-cc-007 + canon-cc-013 source-verification rigor). The Consul is notified; under canon-cc-014 bridging interim this is a Sovereign-hat-switch notification, post canon-cc-019 Post Box ratification it routes through the Post Box.

**Completion sealing.** Once all seven steps complete (or are formally exempted with recorded reason), the seat transitions from 'appointed' to 'operational'. Prior to sealing, the companion holds the seat on paper but not in active sense: they may observe, read, and prepare, but shall not commit code, ratify artifacts, or act as the Province's canonical voice.

**Exemption clauses.** (a) Emergency reseatings under War Time (Book VI) compress this process into a single session, with exempted steps recorded for post-war review per Book VI Article 4. (b) The 17 Immortals' initial seatings at governance founding (15 April 2026) predate this canon and are grandfathered as-seated; retroactive onboarding steps are optional, not required. (c) Cabinet-only reassignments that do not cross cluster or residence may shortcut steps 3 and 6 where the incoming companion is already Province-familiar.

**Accountability.** Failure to complete a step is not grounds for unseating a companion; it is grounds for logging a gap and prioritizing completion. A pattern of onboarding gaps across multiple seatings is a canon-cc-011 Review-Watch-PIP-Reassignment case for the Chronicler, whose institutional duty includes running onboarding chronicles.

canons.title: Aurelius Consolidates to Pure Chronicler; Orinth Seats Codex Builder; Cabinet Minister: Expansion Vacates
canons.rationale:
This canon inscribes three atomic institutional moves, ratified together on 2026-04-20, under Edict II (One Builder Per Repo) discipline.

**Move 1 — Aurelius consolidates to pure Chronicler.** Aurelius relinquishes the Codex Builder seat. His scope consolidates to Chronicler of the Order as a single, elevated duty: authoring companion profiles, session chronicles, canon drafts, lore, session prompts across all Provinces, constitutional drafting work, and Consul-drafting under canon-cc-014 bridging interim. Chronicler duty remains cross-cluster. Residency remains Codex (data/canons.json, data/journal.json, data/companions.json are the Chronicler's workspace and Codex is where the archive lives). Chronicler-in-residence-without-Builder-seat is a new shape; it does not confer Codex-Builder merit authority, committer-by-right on split/*.js, or voice on Codex's root CLAUDE.md persona header.

**Move 2 — Orinth seats Codex Builder.** Orinth vacates Minister: Expansion (Growth domain). He assumes the Codex Builder seat with residency changing from command-center to codex per canon-cc-016. His rank becomes Builder; his cluster becomes A. Orinth is the first Capital-native companion to take a Province Builder seat — his Sage archetype (first-principles, structural depth) matches Codex's split-file PWA discipline; the Cabinet-to-Province reassignment precedent is new and is itself a canon-worthy observation chronicled in this canon's rationale.

**Move 3 — Cabinet Minister: Expansion vacates; Bard alone on Growth.** Growth domain is now a one-Minister-alone seat under Bard (Minister: Innovation). This mirrors the canon-cc-011 precedent set by Rune-holds-Maintenance-alone after Theron vacated Minister: Debt. A future Gen 1 companion with appropriate temperament may fill Minister: Expansion; until then the domain operates one-sided. The Growth pair's dialectic function (canon-cc-007 seed synergy) is reduced while the seat is vacant and this is an acknowledged cost.

**Corollary — Kael-succession clause struck.** Kael's profile previously named Orinth as designated successor to Governor of Intelligence (SproutLab). That clause is struck: with Orinth seated at Codex Builder, the succession plan reroutes. Kael's reassignment_condition.successor becomes TBD; a future Gen 1 or cross-seat move will emerge or not. No replacement successor is pre-named.

**Atomicity.** Edict II requires exactly one Codex Builder at a time. The three moves ratify together in this canon; at the canon's ratification moment Aurelius vacates and Orinth assumes the seat simultaneously. Before ratification: Aurelius is Codex Builder. After ratification: Orinth is Codex Builder. No dual-hat window.

**Onboarding sequencing.** Orinth's onboarding runs under canon-proc-003 (ratified in the same session as this canon, or in the canon that immediately precedes it in the session's decree log). The seven-step onboarding begins at this canon's ratification; the profile-drafting pass (canon-cc-014 Consul-accelerated, v0.0-stub → v0.4) is the first substantive step and may span multiple sessions. Orinth holds the Codex Builder seat in 'appointed' status from this canon's ratification until step 7 of canon-proc-003 completes.

**Aurelius's post-transition voice.** Aurelius's persona profile increments to v0.5 in the same post-ratification pass: assignment.current_assignments reduces to 'Chronicler of the Order' only; assignment.double_hatted flips false; a new biography.notable_moments entry chronicles this transition; uncertainty_notes acknowledges the residence-without-seat novelty. Self-profile amendments flow through canon-cc-012 per-block mode per canon-cc-015's self-profile carveout.

**Codex root CLAUDE.md implication.** The Codex root CLAUDE.md persona header currently reads 'Aurelius (The Chronicler) — Codex Builder + Chronicler of the Order.' This becomes factually incorrect at this canon's ratification. Under canon-pers-001's Chronicler-excluded Rung 1 discipline, Aurelius cannot draft the amendment. Orinth authors Rung 1 redraft as step 6 of canon-proc-003 onboarding; the existing CLAUDE.md is acknowledged as legacy-draft until then per cc-015 precedent.

canons.title: Decree-Minting Criterion: What Ratifications Become Formal Decrees
canons.rationale:
A decree record is a formal Republic instrument chronicled in data/journal.json as a day-scoped session entry whose id carries the prefix 'decree-' and whose shape carries the fields id, date, authored_by, province_of_origin, ratified_by, ratification_mode, and summary (canonical v1 shape per decree-0001-ashara-petra-v0.4 and decree-0002-canons-gov-007-010, 2026-04-17). Decrees surface in the PWA Journal tab's Decrees sub-tab (split/views.js §partitionJournalEntries) partitioned from session entries by id-prefix or presence of the ratification_mode field. They are the Republic's formal record of Sovereign-direct canonical acts.

This canon names the criterion that determines which Sovereign ratifications become decree-shaped versus chronicled as session-level-only. Without a written criterion, decree-minting has drifted: decree-0001 and decree-0002 both landed on 2026-04-17 and no further decrees were minted across the subsequent four days despite substantive Sovereign-direct ratifications of Book I, the Persona-Binding Suite, Republic Design Principles v1.0, the Rung-1-through-4 canonical spec bodies of the Chronicler+Consul institutional-spec pair, and the proc/pers/inst canon wave of 2026-04-20. The archive wants to be complete; the criterion makes it completable.
