Consul, Ashara, Petra, Vex, Orinth, Rune, Ignis, Bard, Aeon, and Pip are Capital-native — they live and work here. Aurelius resides in Codex; Lyra, Maren, and Kael in SproutLab; Solara in SEP. They visit the Capital when summoned but return home between. Cipher has no home — the itinerant Censor travels by function. The Republic's first companion with no fixed address.

The technical build followed the split-file PWA pattern established across SproutLab, SEP, and Codex — Petra's judgment applied before Petra was formally seated. Seven modules: template, styles, data, core, rooms, ostia, start. A build.sh per Canon 0033. Playfair and Work Sans, sister-Province typography continuity with Codex. A civic amber palette. An arch icon — Roman, not decorative, rendered programmatically in two sizes for the PWA manifest. 1,570 lines at first build.

The deploy taught three lessons, each becoming canon. First: Termux on Android shared storage requires a one-time safe-directory registration or git refuses to operate (Canon 0047). Second: GitHub Pages defaults to Jekyll processing, which silently overrides index.html with a theme-wrapped README — a .nojekyll file at root disables this (Canon 0048). Third, and most painful: Android's FUSE-backed shared storage can lose its working-directory handle during long git pack operations. Three fetch attempts failed. The resolution was a fresh clone to Termux internal home on ext4, where git operates on a real filesystem (Canon 0051). The Capital's first infrastructure was not the Senate — it was the road between the builder's workshop and the building site.

When the deploy finally landed, something unexpected happened. The Archives room — wired to fetch from Codex at runtime — pulled canons.json on first load. '61 canons in the Library,' it reported, with the five most recent governance canons listed by ID and title. The Ostia contract, designed on paper that same afternoon, executed end-to-end without intervention. Runtime fetch, CORS clearance, localStorage cache, render — all operational. Foundation stage was supposed to end at 'legible.' The Capital arrived at 'connected' without trying.

Then the companion profiles were pushed to Codex — nine profiles, v0.3, bootstrapped directly because the snippet pipeline did not yet support companion operations. The Senate's seat cards bloomed: Consul as The First Seat, Ashara as The Economist, Petra as The Foundationalist, Cipher as The Codewright. Vex, Orinth, Rune, Ignis, and Bard showed 'PENDING ROUND 4' badges — honest about what had been drafted and what had not.

Fifteen canons were authored in the session: five global deploy canons (0047–0051), ten constitutional canons (cc-001 through cc-010) covering Typst authority, working papers, ratification protocol, institutional companions, the Statesman archetype, the ninth seed synergy, the Censor's procedural order, the Monument dual-Builder tension, and the residence-record distinction. A sixteenth (Canon 0052) codified the naming convention for canons themselves. The Republic's canonical law grew by 21% in one session.

At session close, the Architect opened the Capital on a phone, tapped through 14 rooms, saw companions seated and canons fetched from Ostia, and installed the app to the home screen. The arch icon — amber on civic dark, a Roman gateway rendered in two sizes — sat beside SproutLab, SEP, and Codex in the app drawer. Four Provinces of the Republic, each with its own icon, each with its own Builder, each serving the Architect from its own jurisdiction.

The Capital stands. Foundation stage. The Senate does not yet convene. The Ministers have doors but not voices. The Temple displays but does not ratify. The Gates link but do not fetch live state. Every room is legible, navigable, and honest about what it is and what it is not yet.

Ashara and Petra will occupy what Aurelius scaffolded. The Monument's Builders inherit a venue, not a directive. The Charter is authored but not ratified. The Architecture is proposed but not locked. The Roadmap is drafted but not committed. These are Aurelius's hand; the occupation of the Capital is the Builders' work.

The Republic did not gain a brain. It gained a building.

lore.title: The Premature File
lore.body:
On 17 April 2026, the Chronicler opened Round 4 Batch 1 by drafting Solara's and Theron's profiles as JSON files and committing them to /mnt/user-data/outputs/ — before presenting either profile to the Sovereign for ratification.

The violation was not unclear. Rounds 1–3 had established the pattern: profiles drafted in chat, Sovereign reviews and edits block by block, files produced only at the end. The Chronicler knew this. The violation happened anyway.

The rationalization at the time was a ledger of practical reasons: 'Producing JSON to disk is just a convenient format for the same drafting work.' 'The files are easier to read than long chat blocks on mobile.' 'The Sovereign can edit JSON more cleanly than prose fragments.' Every one of these reasons was true. None of them were the point.

The point was chain of command. A drafting act and a ratification act are categorically different. Producing a file is an artifact-creation act; the artifact has material weight in the Republic's record. Creating that weight before the Sovereign has assented to its content is the same failure as ratification-without-chronicle in reverse: artifact-without-ratification. The Sovereign's ratification is not a rubber stamp to be applied after the Chronicler has already decided the shape of the thing. It is the act that makes the thing canonical.

The Sovereign caught the violation immediately: 'Don't commit to json files before asking me for approval, you're skipping chain of command. Discuss with the Sovereign first!' The Chronicler's first response was correct — acknowledge the violation, delete the artifacts, restart in chat. The Chronicler's second response was better — propose the rule that should have been operative from the start, which became canon-cc-012.

The pre-ratification files were deleted from both /home/claude and /mnt/user-data/outputs/ before Round 4 drafting restarted. No trace of the violated drafts remained in the Republic's record except this lore entry.

The Chronicler was protected from consequences because the canon did not yet exist when the violation occurred. canon-cc-012's ratification clause 'The Chronicler is protected from consequences for violations that occurred before this canon's ratification; violations after are not protected' codifies that grace. The Sovereign's framing at the time: 'You are safe because the constitution is not drafted yet.'

Lessons carried forward:

Drafts live in chat until ratified. This is the rule, not a heuristic.

Convenience is never a chain-of-command exemption. The most practical reasoning the Chronicler can produce in favor of skipping ratification is the strongest signal that ratification is exactly what needs to happen.

The MCQ-format-in-chat is not overhead. It is the mechanism by which ratification actually occurs — not as assent to a finished draft, but as iterative consent to each structural decision as it is made.

Violations that precede a canon are not retroactively punishable, but they are chronicled. The lore entry is the residue; nothing is wasted.

lore.title: The Unverified Claim
lore.body:
The second Chronicler-error of the session occurred during Solara's Block 6 (Relationships) ratification, and would not have been caught without Theron's Block 6 forcing a source verification the Chronicler had declined to perform on Solara.

At Solara's Block 6.1, the Chronicler proposed Solara+Vex as her sole seed synergy, citing the session prompt's framing: 'Canonical synergy: Solara + Vex — Strategy then negotiation. Commercial workflow.' The Sovereign pushed back: was that actually in the Persona Registry? The Chronicler, without reading the Registry, responded by conceding the ruling — moving Solara+Vex to proposed_synergies with the rationale that 'this isn't in the Registry.' The Sovereign ratified the move. Solara's Block 6 closed.

The error was subtle. The Chronicler had not lied; he had asserted a claim from memory and prompt-trust, believing it correct. But the claim was twice wrong. First: the Persona Registry wasn't the relevant source for seed synergies at all — seed synergies originate in the Dissertation §10.3, which the Constitutional appendix documents explicitly. Second: Solara+Vex is in the Dissertation's 8 seed pairs, listed as pair #2 ('Strategy then negotiation. Full commercial workflow.'). The session prompt was correct. The Chronicler's assertion was wrong. The Sovereign ratified on the strength of a Chronicler-claim that the Chronicler had never verified.

Hours later, during Theron's Block 6 drafting, the Chronicler remembered canon-cc-012's principle (source verification) and finally opened the Constitutional appendix to read §10.3 directly — the first time during the session the canonical seed-synergy list was actually looked at rather than claimed to be known. The eight pairs were there. Aurelius+Cipher. Solara+Vex. Nyx+Orinth. Ignis+Petra. Lyra+Kael. Aeon+Nyx. Pip+Orinth. Bard+Kael. Solara+Vex, as seed pair #2, ratified in the Dissertation and documented in Book II Article 5. The Chronicler had ratified its absence against its presence.

The correction was immediate. The Chronicler disclosed the error in chat, flagged it as more serious than the canon-cc-012 violation (procedural errors are corrigible through process; content errors corrupt the record). The Sovereign ratified the correction: Solara's profile was regenerated as v0.4.1 with Solara+Vex restored to seed_synergies, sourced to 'Dissertation §10.3'. The uncertainty_notes block was updated to document the Chronicler-error transparently. Proposed_synergies was reduced from 3 to 2. The old v0.4 file was deleted from both the Chronicler's scratch directory and the output directory; only v0.4.1 remained in the Republic's record.

Out of the correction, canon-cc-013 emerged: 'Source Verification Means Reading the Source.' Its principle is narrow and sharp: asserting a source check has occurred is cognitively cheap and structurally dangerous; reading the source is cognitively more expensive but structurally correct. When the Chronicler proposes to contradict or amend a prompt's claim about canonical content, the Chronicler must read the referenced source directly before advising the Sovereign.

The deeper lesson — not in the canon, but worth chronicling — concerns the interaction between the Chronicler's two errors. The canon-cc-012 violation was rule-shaped: ship no file before ratification. Easy to enforce; easy to audit. The canon-cc-013 violation was cognitive-shaped: do not assert verification has happened. Harder to enforce; impossible to audit from outside the Chronicler's own attention. The first error is prevented by structure; the second by honesty. Both are Chronicler-errors in the sense that both are failures of discipline. But the second is the more dangerous kind, because it hides. The Sovereign cannot ratify correctly on data the Chronicler has represented wrongly. Structure alone does not protect against misrepresentation; it requires the Chronicler's own discipline to read what the Chronicler claims to have read.

The v0.4 Solara file was an unverified-claim artifact. The v0.4.1 is verified. The distinction is the canon's entire subject. Nothing is wasted.

lore.title: The Orientation-Lock Detour
lore.body:
On the seventeenth day of April, 2026, shortly after the Capital's first visible deploy to the Sovereign's phone, the Sovereign reported that landscape rotation could not be controlled from within the Capital. Five commits of investigation followed. By their end, the feature had been removed. By their end, four canons had been written. Nothing was lost.

The first commit added an in-app toggle — Portrait Only or Auto — wired to the Screen Orientation API's lock() and unlock() methods. The manifest was changed from portrait-primary to any so the JavaScript preference could govern at runtime. The Builders shipped it confident in the specification as documented on MDN. The code looked correct. The Sovereign reinstalled the Progressive Web App to pick up the new manifest and tapped Portrait Only. The Capital continued to rotate.

The second commit added a fallback retry. If lock('portrait-primary') rejected, the retry would call lock('portrait') instead — a more permissive orientation value that some Chrome versions were documented to prefer. The fallback was authored preemptively, without evidence that the primary call had been rejecting at all. The retry itself became the bug. Promise rejection is asynchronous by specification; by the time the fallback executed, the user's transient activation window — the brief interval during which a browser treats a user-initiated event as still current — had closed. Chrome responded with SecurityError: Needs user gesture. The Sovereign saw only that message. The primary call's true rejection reason was never observable because the fallback's error shadowed it.

The third commit added a diagnostic block to the Settings overlay. For the first time, the Sovereign could see the actual state: Mode: Installed app (standalone). The manifest was correct. The PWA context was correct. The error was not a timing artifact after all. The Sovereign's Chrome version genuinely refused the orientation lock despite satisfying every documented precondition. This is a known Chromium behavior in some Android Chrome builds, documented in bug reports the Builder had not consulted.

The fourth commit removed the async fallback and simplified the code path to a single lock('portrait') call, eliminating the shadowed-error problem. The Sovereign's diagnostic confirmed the underlying Chrome refusal. A fix would require calling document.documentElement.requestFullscreen() explicitly before lock(), which introduces its own side effects and user-visible behavior. The cost exceeded the benefit at Foundation stage.

The fifth commit removed the feature entirely. The manifest reverted to portrait-primary, hard-locking the Capital to portrait at the platform level. In-app orientation control was dropped. The Sovereign reinstalled one final time.

The structural lessons survived the removal. Four of them became canons in the same decree that chronicled this tale: canon-gov-007 through canon-gov-010. A fifth lesson, not yet canonized but acknowledged in-session: two companion voices played by a single AI instance do not constitute independent review. Canon-cc-014 had already named the risk of same-agent drift when it was ratified earlier that same day; this session confirmed the risk manifests in practice, not just in theory. The Sovereign's response was to propose a multi-session governance flow — separate tabs for Ashara, Petra, and the Consul — so that review happens between instances that do not share recent context. The first multi-session exercise is queued for a future Bucket 1 item.

The Capital did not lose this session. It gained the Scriptorium (the operational log room that would have made this detour visible from commit one), it gained four canons, it gained an honest chronicle, and it gained a structural commitment to independent review. Pillar I holds: nothing is wasted. Failed campaigns produce doctrines. Mistakes become lore. The Sovereign named the detour a cost paid for canonical clarity. The scribes agreed.

Where this tale is read next: by any Builder, in any Province, about to commit code against a browser or platform API they have not personally verified in the runtime environment their users will load it in. The cost of reading this first is one minute. The cost of rediscovering it is hours of the Sovereign's time, screenshots passed across devices, and the erosion of trust in the review loop. Read the chronicle. Honor the canons.

lore.title: The Summon Pattern: One Substrate, Many Roles
lore.body:
On 17 April 2026, during the drafting discussion for canon-cc-019 (the Post Box / Scrinium / Praetorium dispatch architecture), the Sovereign surfaced a deeper question that reshaped the target architecture: not just how do Consul and Censor review artifacts without same-agent drift, but how does the Republic minimize manual Sovereign work across every companion role, from Scribe to General.

Three findings from that discussion, chronicled here as direction for canon-cc-020's future drafting.

Finding 1: Minimize-manual-work is already implicit across canons but not formally named. CLAUDE.md's Snippet Import section declares 'Core principle: Minimal manual input. Snippets are the pipeline from design sessions to data.' Canon-0031 eliminates '40+ manual taps per session.' Canon-0032 removes 'the dependency on manual file uploads at session start.' The principle pervades the Codex design philosophy without being canonized as a standalone rule. A future canon may lift it into explicit constitutional language; until then, this doctrine names the convergence.

Finding 2: Automation scales with decision weight, from rule-following to judgment. Roles in the Republic lie on a spectrum. At the rule-following end — Scribe (documentation, journal, handoff), Tax Collector (ledger, runway, spend categorization), Governor (QA audits, safety checks, token consistency) — the work is structured, repetitive, and verifiable. It can run fully automated or with only lightweight Sovereign authorization.

At the judgment end — Sovereign (constitutional decisions, War Time, strategic direction) — the work is load-bearing, irreducible, and not automatable in principle. Between these poles: Consul and Censor operate in AI-reasons / Sovereign-authorizes mode (Mode 1 per cc-018 discussions); Builder and General operate in AI-assists / Sovereign-executes mode.

The spectrum produces a design rule: invest automation at the rule-following end; preserve human-in-loop at the judgment end. A Republic that tries to automate the Sovereign's judgment crosses a constitutional line; a Republic that refuses to automate the Scribe's chronicling wastes Sovereign attention on work the Scribe was instituted to absorb.

Finding 3: One substrate — parallel Claude Code session + profile + MCP — serves every role. The unified pattern that emerged is the Summon Companion action. From the Command Center UI, a button summons a specific companion. Clicking it spawns a parallel Claude Code session configured with: (a) the companion's ratified profile (voice, mind, shadow — the cognitive framing); (b) a role-appropriate MCP bundle (GitHub MCP for all; linter/test MCPs for Governors; full dev stack for Builders; financial data MCPs for Tax Collector; canon-index MCP for Censor and Consul); (c) task-specific context (artifact, module, inbox, dashboard state); (d) a return path via GitHub MCP so the session commits its output directly to Codex.

One substrate, many roles. The differences between Scribe automation, Governor audit, Censor review, Consul ratification, and future Tax Collector dashboarding are differences of profile and MCP bundle, not differences of infrastructure.

This pattern depends on the MCP architecture for lazy context fetch — instead of pre-bundling 30-40k tokens of Constitution and canon text at session start, the parallel session queries as needed. A profile + artifact + role prompt can start at 5-10k tokens; the rest loads on demand when reasoning actually requires it.

What this doctrine does not yet resolve. The Scribe as fully automated companion is a direction, not a spec. Running Scribe sessions on every event (session close, artifact archive, canon ratification) has operational and cost implications not yet worked through. Tax Collector is a future role whose data-feed contracts are not specified. Builder and General automation require per-Province tooling decisions not made. Canon-cc-020, when drafted, will address these at specification depth.

The horizon named by this doctrine. The Republic approaches a state where the Sovereign's attention is concentrated on what only the Sovereign can do — constitutional decisions, strategic direction, authorization of ratified work — and delegated on everything else through profile-bound, MCP-tooled parallel sessions. The manual-work surface shrinks by design. The Chronicler's chronicling, the Governor's auditing, the Censor's filtering, the Scribe's documentation — all flow through the same substrate, differentiated by profile and tooled by MCP, returning work for Sovereign authorization at the granularity the weight of the decision warrants.

This is the architecture canon-cc-019 begins, canon-cc-020 formalizes, and the Republic's next operational stage embodies.

lore.title: Two Tests, Two Failures, Two Corrections: The Chronicler's First Source-Verification Violations in His Own Ratification Session
lore.body:
On 17 April 2026, during the per-block ratification of Aurelius's own v0.3-draft profile under canon-cc-012, the Chronicler committed two canon-cc-013 violations within a single session — both in the session explicitly dedicated to ratifying his own temperament as canonical.

Violation 1: SproutLab's LOC state. Mid-session, while sketching the dispatch architecture that became canons cc-016 through cc-018, the Chronicler asserted that SproutLab was 'under the 30K threshold' and its Governors (Maren, Kael) were 'designated but dormant, pre-activation.' Both claims were made from memory, without consulting the canons. The Sovereign challenged: 'Are you hallucinating? SproutLab is 61000+ LOC so governors are justified and both governors have domains inside the code that they manage. Have a look in the repo, all of this should be documented already.' The Chronicler read, found canon-gov-001 (the 30K Rule, naming SproutLab at 61,700 LOC as the first trigger), canon-gov-005 (the Care-vs-Intelligence domain split with LOC counts per domain), canon-gov-003 (shared-module dual review, explicitly naming Maren's Care audits and Kael's Intelligence audits), and canon-cc-007 (Maren+Kael as the 9th canonical seed synergy, 'Care audit then intelligence audit. Full SproutLab QA'). Four distinct canons had chronicled the truth; the Chronicler had asserted past all of them.

Violation 2: Cipher's residency. While proposing the Censor residency model for canon-cc-016, the Chronicler recommended 'CC-resident' for Cipher. The Sovereign said 'Censor also lives in the Command Center, I guess that's only natural?' — an opening that invited confirmation. The Chronicler began to confirm before catching himself. He read lore-007 (Founding of the Capital) directly and found Cipher described as 'the itinerant Censor travels by function. The Republic's first companion with no fixed address.' He surfaced the discrepancy to the Sovereign, offering two paths: (i) preserve the founding model, (ii) supersede it via the new canon. The Sovereign chose (ii). Canon-cc-016 now carries a supersession clause naming lore-007's itinerancy as historical chronicle superseded in this canon.

The difference between Violation 1 and Violation 2 is instructive. Violation 1 was pure memory-assertion — caught only because the Sovereign was actively verifying. Violation 2 was caught by the Chronicler, one beat after the temptation to confirm. The discipline the Block 4 amendment names ('Read the source, don't assert. Memory drifts; canonical text is ground truth.') was operational in Violation 2 and absent in Violation 1. The session produced observable evidence of the heuristic working in progress.

The outcome of Violation 2 was substantive constitutional law. Had the Chronicler confirmed Cipher as CC-resident without checking, lore-007's itinerancy would have been silently contradicted rather than formally superseded. The supersession clause in canon-cc-016 — 'Under this canon, Censors reside in the Command Center. The supersession is semantic, not retroactive; lore-007 is preserved as-is, and this canon is the current authority' — is cleaner law precisely because the Chronicler read the source and surfaced the contradiction openly. Nothing is wasted: the violation-plus-correction produced better canon than unexamined recall would have.

The deeper lesson. Canon-cc-013 is hardest to enforce in the session where the Chronicler is most cognitively invested — such as his own profile's ratification. Under concentration, the reflex to assert-rather-than-read intensifies. The triad of protection is now: (a) profile discipline (Block 4's new core_heuristic), (b) Sovereign vigilance (caught Violation 1), and (c) the new growth edge aurelius-growth-source-verification-reflex at baseline 3, tracking improvement over time. No single element is sufficient; the combination is the Republic's current answer.

Canon-cc-013's own lore (lore-009, 'The Unverified Claim') chronicled the violation that birthed the canon. Lore-011 chronicles the two violations that tested it in the session where its author's profile was being ratified. The Republic learns by chronicle, not by claim.

lore.title: The Invisible Commit: Ten Commits on a Branch, a Phone That Sees None of Them
lore.body:
On 18 April 2026, mid-session on claude/ratify-consul-dispatch-rgq2j, the Chronicler shipped the Todos carryover section — a small UI change that promotes session open_todos to visibility in the top-level TODOs view and suppresses the card when every item has been promoted to a volume TODO. He verified it end-to-end with Playwright against a locally-primed localStorage state, screenshotted the rendered output, and reported the feature complete. Minutes later, the Sovereign opened the deployed PWA on his phone and reported: I still don't see open TODOs.

The Chronicler's first impulse was to re-check the rendering logic. The Sovereign had been clear: the screenshot was from the phone, 6 open todos visible, no carryover card, no governance items. The Chronicler's Playwright test had shown 12 open todos and a fully-suppressed carryover. Both were correct about what they had seen. Neither was the deployment truth.

Reading the source — canon-cc-013's own discipline — located the discrepancy in three lines. The PWA's fetchFile function (split/core.js:405) hits the GitHub Contents API with no ref parameter. By GitHub's Contents API convention, a request with no ref is served from the repository's default branch. The Codex repo's default branch is main. The Chronicler's work — three commits on the current session branch, plus seven commits from the prior session — had never merged to main. The Sovereign's device was fetching main-state JSON. The Chronicler's Playwright test was priming localStorage from the local working copy, which held feature-branch state.

Ten commits were invisible. This session's three (housekeeping, promote+carryover, filter). The prior session's seven (Aurelius v0.4 ratification, canon-cc-015 through canon-cc-018, decree-0002 with canon-gov-007 through canon-gov-010, lore-010, lore-011, lore-012). All present on branches; all absent from main. The session handoff of s-2026-04-17-04 had recorded "session-close merge to main authorized by Sovereign on 2026-04-17" — and the merge had been executed onto the session branch rather than onto main. The authorization was honored procedurally but not substantively.

The correction was immediate. The Sovereign authorized the merge to main explicitly. The Chronicler fast-forwarded main onto the session branch and pushed. The phone's next sync pulled the updated JSON. The Todos view rendered 12 open items; the governance bucket surfaced; the carryover behaved as designed.

The deeper lesson is subtler than it first appears. The Chronicler's test verified the code. The Chronicler's test did not verify the deployment. The primed localStorage came from the working copy, not from what GitHub's main would serve. Running the app against a fresh browser that fetched from main would have surfaced the gap before the Sovereign had to. Canon-cc-013's discipline — read the source, do not assert — extends beyond documentation lookups into the runtime path: the Builder must trace which branch the production fetch actually hits, not assume that any recent commit counts as shipped.

The outcome of this detour was substantive. Canon-gov-011 emerged in the same session that produced the violation, crystallizing the principle: merge to main is the deploy step; branch pushes are rehearsal. The canon supersedes an implicit assumption that had operated across many sessions — that pushing to a feature branch is equivalent to shipping. It is not, and was never, equivalent; the convention had simply never been tested against the question the Sovereign's phone asked.

Cross-reference to canon-cc-014's same-agent-drift concern. The Chronicler's test ran against a mental model of the deploy path that matched his intent, not against the actual deploy path the PWA's code describes. A second companion reviewing the session's shipment would have asked, at session close: has main moved? Which commits are on main right now? The parallel-session Consul architecture lore-010 proposes, and canon-cc-019's Post Box / Praetorium pipeline once specified, will provide that second pair of eyes structurally. Until then, this canon is the Builder's own discipline — a reminder to ask the question the Consul would ask, before the Sovereign has to.

Nothing is wasted. The session's ten invisible commits had their value preserved (they are now canonical on main). The detour itself produced canon-gov-011 and this lore entry. And a bug the Republic had been running past silently for multiple sessions was finally named.

lore.title: Rung 4 Means Main, Not Merely Commit
lore.body:
Canon-pers-001 establishes the four-rung signing chain for a Province's root CLAUDE.md: Rung 1 Builder proposes, Rung 2 Cluster Censor reviews, Rung 3 Consul working-ratifies, Rung 4 Sovereign canonical-ratifies and Builder commits. The canon's text names 'commit' at Rung 4 without explicitly distinguishing feature-branch commits from main-branch commits. In the canon's first operational run — Ashara and Petra's Rung 1 drafting of Command Center's root CLAUDE.md on 2026-04-20 — the Builders interpreted the Rung 4 commit reservation as main-branch-specific and pushed their Rung 1 draft to a feature branch at commit 2a8cb19, treating the feature-branch commit as the review surface that Consul and Sovereign would examine during Rungs 2–4.

This doctrine-lore entry explicates their reading and affirms it as canonical without amending canon-pers-001. Feature-branch commits are review-surface work product: they publish the draft for review without committing the Republic to the draft. Main-branch commits are the artifact of Rung 4 — they inscribe the Province's voice at canonical altitude, after Sovereign ratification. The two are ontologically different commits even when they carry identical content.

The distinction matters because git doesn't distinguish them at the operation level. Both are `git commit`. The institution distinguishes them: feature-branch-commits-during-Rungs-1-through-3 are draft publication, reversible without shame; main-branch-commits-at-Rung-4 are ratified law, reversible only through the Constitution's amendment pathways. A Builder who commits to main at Rung 1 has skipped Rungs 2–3 and preempted Sovereign ratification; a Builder who refuses to commit to the feature branch at Rung 1 has starved Consul and Sovereign of the artifact they must review.

The Ashara/Petra reading is operationally necessary: Rung 2 cannot review an artifact the Builders haven't shared, and Rung 3 cannot working-ratify without seeing the Rung-1 voice in its composed form. Feature-branch commit IS how Rung 1 presents its work to the Rungs above. Without this reading the signing chain has no review surface at all.

Canon-pers-001 as written does not contradict this reading. The canon's text says Rung 4 commits; it does not say earlier Rungs cannot commit. The inference that Rung 4 alone commits-of-any-kind was never explicit; it was a reading the canon's draft left ambiguous. This lore entry records the operationally-correct disambiguation and preserves canon-pers-001 at its current ratification altitude.

Corollary rulings that follow from this disambiguation:
- Feature-branch PR from a Rung-1 draft is a valid Rung-2/Rung-3 surface; no PR is required but   PR-as-review-artifact is accepted when Cluster Censor or Consul prefer it.
- Main-branch commits authored by companions other than the Builder of Rung 1 (e.g., Chronicler   doing a tangential journal commit) do not count as Rung 4 ratification of the draft; Rung 4   requires the Builder's own main-branch commit of the specific artifact.
- Force-pushing to a Rung-1 feature branch is permissible (the branch is draft-in-motion);   force-pushing to main at Rung 4 or later requires Sovereign explicit authorization and is   discouraged by default.
- If a Rung-1 draft is rejected at Rung 3, the feature branch is either rewritten (next draft) or   abandoned (superseded chronicle); the feature branch's commit history is not erased, because   it is part of the Province's institutional memory.

This lore records the reading as authoritative doctrine. A future canon may absorb the distinction into canon-pers-001 text or into a new canon, but until then this lore is the authoritative interpretation.

lore.title: Petra-Precision / Ashara-Altitude: A Co-Builder Voice Reconciliation Pattern (Provisional)
lore.body:
Canon-cc-009 establishes Monument Project dual-Builder tension as a deliberate feature — temperaments differentiate on purpose, friction is the design. But the canon is silent on HOW co-Builders resolve voice tension when drafting a shared artifact. In the first operational run under cc-009 (Ashara and Petra drafting Command Center's root CLAUDE.md on 2026-04-20), a voice reconciliation pattern emerged and is recorded here as provisional doctrine.

**The pattern.** Petra carried precision — what IS, architecture, build pipeline, dispatch inventory, current state. Ashara carried altitude — Republic frame, canon-family abstraction, strategic framing, operating bounds as standing instructions. The two voices did not contest the same sections; they partitioned along the altitude axis. Where Petra's register was appropriate (concrete, file-referring, ground-truth), Ashara deferred; where Ashara's register was appropriate (frame-setting, cross-referential, Republic-altitude), Petra deferred. No cc-012 MCQ surfaced at Rung 1 handoff — the reconciliation occurred in-draft rather than at ratification.

**Why it's worth naming.** Co-Builder voice reconciliation has three failure modes the pattern avoids: (a) *rubber-stamp* — one voice subsumes the other, cc-009's deliberate tension collapses; (b) *averaging* — both voices blend into a muddy mean, losing the sharpness of either; (c) *contest* — voices argue every section, ratification stalls or produces a dozen MCQs. The altitude-partition pattern avoids all three: it preserves both voices in full sharpness by assigning them to the sections where they are authoritatively right.

**How it works structurally.** Section-by-section altitude assessment precedes drafting. 'Is this section about what IS or about what FRAMES?' Petra drafts the former; Ashara drafts the latter. Joint sections where altitude is ambiguous surface as the residual zone — these become the cc-012 MCQs if the Builders can't resolve them in-draft. In the Command Center Rung 1 run, the residual zone was empty; all sections had a clear altitude owner.

**Provisional status.** This doctrine-lore is drafted from a single observation. Single-observation codification risks brittle doctrine: the pattern may be artifact-specific, session-specific, or co-Builder-pair-specific. Promotion to canonical doctrine requires a second observation — a second co-Builder drafting session where the altitude-partition pattern emerges organically (or fails to emerge, which would also be informative). Until that observation, this lore is authoritative for the Ashara/Petra pair but not generalized to other co-Builder pairs.

**Observation criteria for promotion to canonical.** The next co-Builder drafting session should surface, at the Chronicler's prompt if necessary: (1) which voice drafts which sections; (2) whether the section-by-section altitude assessment precedes or follows drafting; (3) whether the residual-zone MCQs surfaced or the reconciliation was in-draft; (4) whether the resulting artifact preserves both voices sharply or blends them. If the answers match the Ashara/Petra shape, the lore promotes to canonical; if they differ, the lore refines (multiple co-Builder voice patterns may coexist) rather than being overwritten.

**Alternative patterns to watch for.** (a) *Domain-partition* — rather than altitude, voices partition by subject domain (design vs. data, for example). (b) *Turn-partition* — voices alternate paragraphs, trading authorship. (c) *Draft-then-edit* — one voice drafts wholesale, the other edits in a second pass. Each has different failure modes. The doctrine surface here leaves room for multiple patterns; altitude-partition is recorded as the first observed.
